sentences.text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
My name-- my name is Romana Radzicki Farrington
F-A-R-R-I-N-G-T-O-N.
Farrington?
No.
Radzicki is my maiden name.
And my name that I was with until my real parents that moved back was Stanczak.
S-T-A-N-- N-- N, yes-- C-Z-A-K.
I was given my name, Romana, by my previous parents, by Stanczak.
By my real parents, I believe my name was Helena.
My birthday is June 9, 1949.
No, sorry.
I am 55 as of yesterday.
It was my birthday yesterday.
I was born in Krosniewice, in the ghetto.
K-R-O-S-N-I-E-W-I-C-E.

Basically the memories that I recall are starting from the time that my real mother came into the room and grabbed me, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] started kissing me, and crying out that I am her daughter.
And this was something horrifying to me, of course.
I run away from her and went into the other room, where my other mother was, which I thought was my real mother.
And I hid myself in her skirt, holding on to her and saying, there is some strange lady in the other room with some other people who tells me I'm her mother.
And that's my recollection.

I do recall certain other things, which was being with my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] grandma, my aunt in the country, when we used to run away, when there were bombings and lots of fire in Lodz, where we lived.
But these were sort of minor recollections, probably some of them due to some nightmares I had also as a child right after the war.
But other than that, this is where, actually, my story begins.
Can you recount your story from your birth until your mother returning?
Can you tell us what-- what-- what happened?
As I understand, my-- my mother, after I was born in Krosniewice Ghetto, stayed with me there with my father and my grandparents for approximately five months until the end of the year.

And from what I understand, when the Germans asked the Jews to pay 8 marks per head in order to be deported, my father felt that it was much better and much more safe for my mother to be in Warsaw Ghetto, since he just could not fathom that they could actually do the same thing so quickly as they did in Krosniewice Ghetto, and close down the ghetto, and get rid of all the Jews.
So he paid one of the ladies that he knew-- smugglers, basically-- who was able to smuggle my mother and myself to Warsaw Ghetto.
And that was in the middle of the war, in the middle of the very, very hard winter, harsh winter.
My mother got to Warsaw Ghetto.
Her sister-- one of her sisters-- I believe Irena-- was there, her older sister.
And my mother moved in with-- with her.
And there were several other people that were not related.
But my aunt was living with her husband at the time.
And from what I understand, we-- we were living there for approximately seven months or so when everybody started saying to my mother that she should run away from there.
But my mother was basically waiting for my father, to hear from him, because he was supposed to run away from Krosniewice Ghetto to [?
Goleniow, ?] where his brother was.
And he wanted to actually prepare the documents in more safe place.
And this is what exactly happened.

So-- however, my mother, not hearing anything from my father and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] worrying about what's-- what's happening, and trying to rescue herself and rescue me-- and especially being told so many times by elder people that she's too young to die-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she decided that she would do that.
As a matter of fact, from what I understand, they had the theater group, which was active in Warsaw Ghetto-- helped my mother, because they helped her financially to escape.
And it's almost miraculous to see how she got out of there.
But sometimes in August, I-- August, I believe, of 1942, she just held me on her arms and she just walked out with me while people were going to work.
So she sort of smuggled herself out with them, with a whole group of people.
And from what I understand, she could have sworn that some of the-- the German must have seen her and-- who was watching the people.
But somehow, he would let her go.
And maybe because she was very pretty, very blonde-- long blonde hair, beautiful lady.
And he just allowed her to escape.
But who knows, you know?
And from what I know, she [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took a horse-drawn carriage to a station, where she could from there go [? to Goleniow. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She arrived at the station.
And she was told that this-- the train just left.

She was very upset because it meant she had to stay for another-- the entire day, till evening-- and worrying that perhaps someone may recognize her or someone may say something, because after all, she was with me.
Didn't carry really any-- anything.
She looked sort of-- she may have looked suspicious.
And she was quite starved at the time, I guess.
And she didn't want to stay at the station.
So she walked out, evidently.

And so a little shop very close by-- and she walked in there and she said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Christ be with you-- which, you know, normal sort of saying, God be with you-- a little Roman Catholic saying, something.
And she said, I am with my daughter going to [?
Goleniow. ?] And the train just left.
And could I possibly stay with her here, because she's a little sick?
And I-- you know, I can't stay at the station with her.
And the lady was very, very nice.
And she said, absolutely, but not here.
You can stay in our room, which is in the back of the store.
And she was very helpful to my mother-- gave her food, gave me food.

And as a matter of fact, at night, she sent her husband with my mother to the train station so she doesn't, you know, look suspicious that she's just going by herself-- not knowing, of course, that she's Jewish, but just not to be by herself, a woman alone with a child.
So the gentleman escorted her to the train.
And then my mother-- luckily, without any documents-- was not stopped, and was able to arrive to the village or small town of [?
Goleniow, ?] where my father's brother was living and where my mother expected that my father would be there by that time.
And when she arrived, my father-- she found out that my father already left.
He was not there.
As a matter of fact, the first thing-- they were so terrified, everybody, when they saw my mother and me.
They said that my father is not there-- he's dead-- and just get out of here.
You know, they just were not very helpful.
And this is the family, you know?
But they all were just terrified.
And one cannot blame them one way or another.
But that's what happened.
And my mother started crying.
Then the mother of-- of my-- of my father's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] mother-in-law of my father's brother came out.
And she says, child, don't cry.
Your husband is fine.
We have documents for you.
And we have documents for us.
And you'll be fine.
Just, you know-- just relax.
And that's it.
And then they decided there was just no way that they would be able to have-- my mother decided.
And they told her she wouldn't be able to go to Germany to work with me.
And so the decision was made that I should be left someplace, you know?
And basically that's what happened.
So the next couple of days, my mother took me and placed me in one of these sort of-- I don't know how you say these-- these things, actually.
It's just sort of like a setup where you put a kid in, and you cover the child, you know, so it won't be cold, etc.
And so she put me into that.
And she also placed on my neck a cross.
And she wrote a letter.
And in the letter-- perhaps she-- I think she left still a few things, whatever she had.
And-- and she wrote a letter specifying that she is in the underground, and the Germans are after her, and they most likely will catch her.
So she would like at least for her daughter to be alive.
So please take her and take care of her.
If I'm-- if I'm going to survive the war, I will come and try to claim her back.
But I doubt very much that I will be a survivor, so please take care of her.
And I did not have a chance yet to christen her, so please christen her, and God be with you.
And that's it.
So my mother was taken, actually, to a small village called Kraszkow [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] by a gentleman who was a friend of the family.
And he went with my mother to that village.
And they went to the village specifically because they were told they were known for helping the underground.
And as a result, they believed that village like that would be a good place for a child to be found and to be safe.
So my mother went with me in the middle of the night with the gentleman.
And he probably arrived to the first home-- not arrived, they walked.
They got to the first home.
And they looked sort of for, I think, one of the best-looking houses so they would feel that perhaps in that particular house, there will be enough food that people would decide to keep me.
And actually, what had happened, it was-- where they left me, it was the home of the village elder, sort of like a mayor of the village.
And so my mother left me.
I was asleep.
It was in middle of the night, dark.
And she and the gentleman just stayed behind the bushes and waited until someone would open the door.
And when that happened, evidently my mother fainted.
And he carried her back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all that I know about that night.

[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] One interesting thing is that following of what happened there, my father's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sister-in-law-- younger sister, who was escaping thanks to my father's documents-- forged documents as well-- went to the station-- to the train station close by of where they were living.
And she was there with one of her nieces and her sister.
And they were going towards [?
Czestochowa-- ?] the same way as my mother was going for work-- to work in Germany.
They were at the station while my other mother was there with me.
And my cousins recognized me.
But of course, they would not come close to me because they do not want to endanger me.
However, they showed-- they saw me.
And they heard the lady say that she just found me.
Someone actually found at the-- the village elderly found me and announced in the church that there is a child, and who would like to take the child?
And she was so delighted to take the child because she only had a son and she couldn't have any more children.
And it was, for her, like a gift from God.
And my cousin said I was beautifully dressed.
And I was going to a doctor with her.
And she was surrounded by some people that she knew.
And she was telling some strangers who were right next-- next to her.
And they were all admiring me.
And they were all happy for her.
And she said she just looked and she was so glad that she could recognize me-- at least that I'm in good hands.
And then she had to get away from there by herself because they started recognizing her sister, that there is a Jew there.
And as a matter of fact, the station master held them out, took them to another room at the time.
And-- and they were able to go to [?
Czestochowa ?] without any problems, but knowing already that I am OK.
Stanczak.
Gnowefa Stanczak was the name of my mother.
The Catholic-- Roman Catholic family, yes.
[SIGHS] Very much.
They were extremely religious people.
And I was to go to church twice a day, in the morning and at-- in the evening.
And it was something which was part of me.
I-- I was brought up having Jesus Christ over my bed, and Mary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was extremely religious little girl.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so you can imagine the shock later on, when I found out actually, that I was not Roman Catholic, but my real parents were Jewish.
It's-- it's very-- it's sort of-- it's very interesting.
But from very early childhood, I-- I did know that Jews killed Jesus.
And that's something which was my god.
And as a result, you know, they were the bad people.
So you're raised with that from-- from when you are a little small child.
And you can't help yourself.
But I disliked Jews as a result of that.
And that-- that has been taught by the priest.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I mean, Roman Catholic religion I think in United States and today is just totally, totally different, because if the Roman Catholics, they knew their religion well, they would know that Jews did not kill Jesus-- plus, that Jesus was Jewish to begin with.
But they would be vehemently against you if you would even suggest something like that.
I mean, it's-- they-- they were very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] uneducated when it really came to their own religion.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you can't help it.
You have these sort of prejudices from a childhood, when you raised in this type of atmosphere.
I don't recall hearing very much about it.

However, my father-- my other father, Stanczak-- was-- he belonged to the so-called Armia Krajowa, AK, which is the military unit-- national military unit which was basically fighting the Germans at first, during the war-- later on, fighting the Russians who came to Poland and fighting the Jews.
And my father was caught, actually, after the war.

That's why my mother had hardly any dealings-- my real mother, when she was looking for me, she had hardly any dealings with my other father because he was always in hiding in post-war Poland, in 1945.
And that's because he was in that group.
They had to hide and they had to support themselves.
And basically, they would support themselves by-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, by going after banks, because that's where the money is.
And he was basically caught robbing a bank, and put in-- into jail in early-- in about-- I think in 1947 or so.
In Krosniewice?
No, in-- in this town where you were with this-- this other family.
I was in Lodz.
That was a large city.
Lodz is probably-- at the time, I think the second-largest city in Poland.
I don't know exactly what its-- what is the ranking of it at the moment, but it's a large city.
And it's only my grandparents, actually-- my other grandparents-- were living in Kraszkow-- in Kraszkow, in the town-- village where my mother left me.
No, no.
Never.
Absolutely not.
And basically, when my mother came after the war, she went straight away right after-- she went straight to the-- the little village where she left me.
And in Kraszkow, she had that little picture, which I showed you previously.
That's the only thing she had.
And she came over to one of the village-- there was a pasture, I believe.
Sheep were on that pasture.
And my mother went over to the shepherdess and asked her, did you-- by any chance, do you know a little girl who was left here back in 1942?
Do you remember her, or do you know where she is?
And the lady-- the old lady said, oh, [?
Romeskza-- ?] that's a diminutive of Romana, which is my name.
And she was-- she looked at the picture which my mother showed her and she says, yes, that's [?
Romeskza. ?] She doesn't live here anymore, but her grandma is here.
And that's how my mother was able to go and see my grandma for the first time.
And through her, she-- she found out where I was.
I don't know personally of what actually-- what did he do.
But I know that he certainly was anti-semite in order to belong to that organization.
And-- but he had no-- he was-- he was really-- other than-- you know, it's-- it's just incredible.
But he was a very loving man-- very very nice person.
And so was the lady who, you know-- his wife.
And she loved me very much and he loved me very much, and also my older brother, who was quite older than me.
And it's a shame, but I just-- I wish I would be able to find him.
And as I've mentioned to you, I just received, about three months ago or two months ago-- I received a letter from Warsaw, where I send a letter through the consulate here.
I got-- I got addresses where I should write to for people who are lost, etc.
And they just told me that there are 50 people of the same name, and they cannot help me.

[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Other than that about my other father, the interesting point is that when he was caught by the Polish government and placed in prison, he stayed there for approximately six years or so.
I don't exactly remember.
But his wife divorced him during the time when he was in prison.
I guess she didn't want to have anything to do with him because of his association.
And she divorced him.
So he didn't really have not any money, not any place to go to.
And I remember him coming to Krosniewice right after he was let out of the jail.
And he stayed with us and with his new girlfriend at the time, or maybe wife already.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
They stayed with us for several days.
And I remember how happy he was to see me and my parents.
And he was so thankful to my parents for the help they-- they gave him, because my father helped him to find a job and gave him money.
And he kissed and hugged my mother and my father.
And he said, I guess Jews are very nice, you know?
So he learned a lot from his experience.
When you say that the priest instilled a sense of anti-Semitism in the people, do you know in-- in what capacity that-- that was done?
I used to go to church continuously, because I was under the 'supervision,' quote unquote, of the Roman Catholic Church till the age of 21.
And I had lived right across from the church.
And I was to go to church in the morning and at night.
And I so much wanted to show everybody that I'm still a Roman Catholic.
And this was the only religion I knew, basically.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I heard, for example, the priest during Christmastime-- that's-- that's the most horrifying thing for me, was when the priest, during-- during the time of Christmas, would just basically say that Jews have-- oh.
Sure.
